entry.title: Understanding Subscription Behavior in the Salesforce Adapter
entry.content:
• Why These Parameters Matter ?
  • Subscription Mechanism
  • Subscription Behaviour on Reset
  • Supported Configuration Combinations
  • Best Practices

 

With the latest enhancement to the Salesforce adapter (version 1.2.0), two powerful configuration parameters have been introduced: 

  • Subscription Mechanism
  • Subscription Behaviour on Reset  

These parameters give you fine-grained control over how events are consumed and how the adapter behaves during restarts, redeployments, or failures. This blog explains each option, when to use it, and recommended best practices. 

Why These Parameters Matter ?

In event-driven integrations with Salesforce, ensuring reliable event consumption is critical. There are several scenarios which can interrupt and thereby impact how subscriptions resume.

  • Error invoked in subscription listener  
  • iFlow redeployment  
  • Tenant restart  
  • Runtime node failure  

These new configurations help you control: 

  • Scalability (multi-node vs single-node)  
  • Data reliability (replay handling)  
  • Failure recovery behavior  

 Subscription Mechanism 

This parameter defines the number of run-time nodes that can participate in event consumption. 

      1. Subscribe on all runtime nodes 

  • All available runtime nodes create subscriptions  
  • Events Processing is distributed across nodes  
  • Designed for high throughput and scalability  

      2. Subscribe on one runtime node 

  • Only a single node actively consumes events  
  • Other nodes remain passive  

      Best for: 

  • Controlled consumption scenarios  
  • Situations where platform limits (like event consumption caps) must be managed  

      Consideration: 

  • If the active node fails, another node must take over → temporary delay possible 

Subscription Behaviour on Reset 

     This parameter defines how the adapter resumes consumption after interruption. 

      1. Replay from UI Configured Value

  • Uses the replay ID configured in the adapter UI  for all subscription creation

      Limitation:   

  • In rare cases can lead to data inconsistency during node failure.

      2. Replay from Last Processed Message 

  • Uses the last processed replay ID  for subscription creation
  • Replay id  are stored in the Integration suite persistence layer  

       Key Benefits: 

  • Ensures no data loss  
  • Automatically resumes from last processed replay id after iFlow redeployment, Tenant restart and temporary failures  

     Important Note: 
      The "Replay ID Approach" configured on the adapter configuration is only used during the first deployment. 
      After that, the  adapter always relies on the persisted replay ID.

Supported Configuration Combinations 

Let’s break down the available combinations and their behavior: 

1. Subscribe on all runtime nodes + Replay from UI Configured Value (Default) 

This is the default configuration where all runtime nodes participate in subscription.

  • All nodes consume events  
  • Replay starts from UI-defined value 

2. Subscribe on all runtime nodes + Replay from Last Processed Message (Recommended) 

This is a recommended configuration where all nodes participate in the subscription and the adapter retains the last successfully received message. In scenarios such as iFlow redeployment or tenant restarts, the adapter resumes the subscription using the stored replay ID rather than the value configured in the UI, thereby ensuring no data loss.

Note: The “Replay ID approach” configuration is applied only during initial deployment. For all subsequent subscriptions, the adapter uses the replay ID stored in the persistence layer to establish new subscriptions. To summarize, this option allows:

  • Multi-node scalability + reliable replay handling  
  • Adapter resumes from last processed message automatically  

Best choice for: 

  • If the interface is not operational in production maintenance window and want to process the replay from the last processed replay id. 
  • Where multi node capability is required for subscribing and consuming event.   

3. Subscribe on one runtime node + Replay from Last Processed Message 

This configuration is suitable for scenarios where multi-node subscription is not required. In a scenario where the Salesforce event consumption count is higher, the “Subscribe on one runtime node” option can be selected. Please note that since the subscription is active on only one node, a node failure may cause a temporary delay in event consumption until another node takes over the subscription.

This setup ensures that event consumption resumes from the last stored replay ID, once the node becomes available again after an unexpected downtime. To summarize, this option allows:

  • Single-node consumption with reliable recovery  
  • Ensures replay continuity using stored replay ID  

Note: The “Replay ID approach” configuration is applied only during the initial deployment. For all subsequent subscriptions, the adapter uses the replay ID stored in the persistence layer to establish new subscriptions.

Suitable for: 

  • High event consumption environments with platform limits  
  • Controlled processing with data safety  

 Note: Node failure may cause temporary delay, but no data loss  

 4. Subscribe on one runtime node + Replay from UI Configured Value (Least Preferred) 

This is the least preferred configuration and should only be used when you need to process events from a specific replay ID, rather than using the value stored in the persistence layer. As the subscription is active on a single node, a node failure may lead to potential event loss, since the replay ID is not persisted. To summarize, this option allows:

  • Single-node + no persisted replay tracking  
  • Subscription always depends on UI configuration  

Use only when: 

  • You must process events from a specific replay ID  

Risks: 

  • Node failure can lead to data inconsistency 

Best Practices

  • Use “Replay from Last Processed Message” wherever possible: This option ensures continuity and prevents data loss. 
  • Prefer multi-node subscription for scalability: Multi-node subscription allows for improved throughput and resilience. 
  • Use single-node subscription only when necessary: This option carries platform-imposed consumption limits and may act as a single point of failure within the system.
  • Avoid UI-based replay together with single-node : This should only be used when you need to process events from a specific replay ID, rather than using the value stored in the persistence layer.

Final Thoughts 

These new configuration options significantly enhance the flexibility of Salesforce event consumption in SAP Integration Suite. 

By choosing the right combination, you can balance: 

  • Performance  
  • Reliability  
  • Operational control  

If configured correctly, they ensure your integration remains resilient, scalable, and loss-free, even during unexpected disruptions.

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: New SAP Champions, Vote Badges Colors and SAP Integration Suite highlights
entry.content:
April has been such a wonderful month, filled with news to be excited about!

From the latest updates in SAP Integration Suite, including the enhanced edition enabling agentic AI, to practical tips on integrating SAP Cloud Integration with SAP Translation Hub and insights to support SAP architecture decisions like CAP vs RAP, there is a lot to explore in this edition.

We also have exciting news from the SAP Community, including the announcement of new SAP Champions and a brand new set of Reply Progression Badges to recognize your engagement.

Have you attended any events from the Brazil CodeJam Roadshow? Today, we are also highlighting one of its biggest milestones: the 60th edition of SAP Inside Track in Brazil! Plus, don’t miss the opportunity to join upcoming community gatherings, including the New York City meetup and SAP Inside Track NRW in the Düsseldorf–Köln area.

Let's dive in!

Featured Blog Posts 

Agentic AI: SAP Integration Suite enhanced edition 

SAP expert Gautham Krishna introduces key information about the enhanced edition of SAP Integration Suite, highlighting the enabling agentic AI in enterprise environments, shifting the conversation from "AI that knows" to "AI that acts." For Gautham, the real challenge is not building AI models, but establishing trust through integration, governance, and secure execution across systems. And with new capabilities like Text-to-Flow, Script Optimization, API Anomaly Detection, and API Traffic Prediction, this enhanced edition helps both accelerate development and strengthen operational reliability. Combined with broader integration services and event-driven architecture, this release positions Integration Suite as the execution layer where AI agents can safely operate, scale, and deliver real business outcomes across SAP and non-SAP landscapes. 

Read Gautham blog post here 

SAP Cloud Integration tips for SAP Translation Hub 

In this practical blog post, SAP Champion Priyanka Chak demonstrates how to integrate SAP Cloud Integration with SAP Translation Hub, using a real-world scenario where error messages are returned in multiple languages, such as English and Japanese. The post walks through the full setup, including creating the Translation Hub service instance, configuring OAuth credentials, and designing an iFlow to connect with the document translation API. It also explains how different translation models can be used, including SAP's Neural Machine Translation and generative AI options for unsupported languages. With clear step-by-step guidance and testing examples, this is a useful prototype for developers looking to enable multilingual capabilities in their integration scenarios. 

Read Priyanka blog post here  

Q1 2026 Highlights for SAP Integration Suite 

Jumping back to SAP Integration Suite, SAP expert Maiara Ellwanger highlights the latest updates for Q1 2026, with a strong focus on improving operations and enabling smoother event-driven integration. As we mentioned previously, one of the key highlights is the introduction of the enhanced edition, but beyond the AI-driven operations and low-code tools, the update also brings improvements in event-driven integration, including better discovery and publishing of events in the Developer Hub and support for migration to Advanced Event Mesh. In addition, new features such as enhanced monitoring, message queue filtering, parameterized mappings, and expanded connectivity options-like customer-managed cloud connectors-aim to simplify troubleshooting and increase flexibility. With new adapters and expanded data center availability, this release delivers practical enhancements for integration of developers and operators working across complex landscapes. 

Read Maiara blog post here  

CAP vs RAP: Really depends? 

SAP Community member, Walter Aguero Machado published a blog post with one of the most common challenges in SAP architecture: deciding between CAP and RAP. The main problem is that for most situations, the answer is "It depends", which is technically correct but ultimately unhelpful in real scenarios. Instead, he shares the practical framework he uses with clients, addressing often overlooked factors such as how team composition influences architecture decisions, what to do with large volumes of legacy Z-objects, and why migrating from RAP to CAP is not a simple refactor. The post clearly explains where RAP genuinely fits, especially for tightly coupled S/4HANA extensions, and where CAP should be the default choice, particularly for multi-source, scalable, and cloud-native scenarios. He also emphasizes a key distinction: while both models can meet Clean Core requirements, CAP enforces it structurally, whereas RAP depends on discipline. This is a very honest and insightful read, and we highly recommend for anyone involved in SAP architecture decisions. 

Read Walter Aguero blog post here 

Community News 

New SAP Champions joining the program!  

Our SAP Community Team is more than happy to announce that we have welcomed 10 new members to the SAP Champions program (learn here more about the program)! Over the past weeks and months we met many outstanding SAP experts - some nominated themselves, others were nominated by peers - and it was inspiring to see the passion and generosity they all bring to the community. 

Choosing this cohort wasn’t easy: every candidate stood out for their consistently positive contributions. We selected experts from around the world who have been sharing knowledge through blog posts, helping others by answering questions in the platform, volunteering as event organizers, and creating impact across SAP Community and other platforms. 

Check out the new SAP Champions, you will soon learn more about them in the SAP Community Leaders Finder as well. 

Tonja Kehrer   

George Drakos  

Simone Azevedo  

Martin Jonen  

Dmitry Kuznetsov  

Yogananda Muthaiah  

Xavier Le Garrec  

Adarsh Rao  

Masashi Otsuka  

Mahesh Palavalli  

Please join us in congratulating our new SAP Champions! The entire SAP Community Team is excited to have you on board and we can’t wait to see what we’ll achieve together. 

We also want to extend a heartfelt thank you to everyone who nominated or was nominated for the program. Your enthusiasm and ongoing contributions - through blog posts, answers, events, mentorship, and more - make the SAP Community stronger every day. Please keep sharing your expertise and supporting others; we truly value every nomination and look forward to seeing more great contributions and nominations in the future. 

Introducing Our New Reply Progression Badges set and Vote for the new Colors! 

We have more news for our SAP Community users: new Reply Progression Badges and new colors for the badges! 

This brand new badge set celebrates your journey as an active community contributor. These badges are earned automatically when you reach specific thresholds of published replies throughout the SAP Community. 

How It Works? It is simple! 

  1. Find content that interests you – Browse across any topic area you’re interested in. 
  1. Share your thoughts – Reply with your insights, experiences, questions, or feedback. Your perspective matters! 
  1. Watch your progress – As your reply count grows, you’ll automatically unlock badges at each milestone. 

These badges are awarded retroactively, which means all your past replies already count. If you’ve been an active community member and have already published 200 replies, you’ll automatically receive all the badges up to that milestone.   

Now for the colors, we need your help!  

We have heard your feedback that the badges all look too similar because they use the same background color. So, the team has decided to update it and we want each badge set to have its own unique color from the SAP brand color palette. Since we have five badge sets to update, we will select the five most popular colors based on your votes.  

How to vote: You just need to comment your choices in the comments section of our SAP Community Admnistrator Caroleigh Deneen blog post here .  On her blog post you will also find more details about each one of the enhancements, and you can also reach out to Caroleigh for any additional questions.  

Events

Brasil CodeJam Roadshow: SIT Campinas Recap 

Last week happened the SAP Inside Track Campinas 2026, the 60th SIT edition in Brazil. Took place at FACAMP in Campinas and brought together an impressive lineup of speakers and community members. The event marked a special moment for the SAP ecosystem in Brazil, with the first visit of SAP Developer Advocates to the country, including @noravonthenen @thomas_jung @RichHeilman @Vitaliy-R and SAP Community managers @antonia_zorn @josh_bentley,. This visit was part of a nationwide SAP CodeJam Roadshow, which hosted more than 17 hands-on sessions across Brazil. The event featured a full-day agenda with sessions on topics like ABAP AI, Generative AI, SAP BTP, and Business Data Cloud, combined with a highly engaging and welcoming atmosphere that kept attendees connected and involved throughout the entire day. Thank you to all the event organizers, volunteers, and attendees. The Brazil community has been truly inspiring for all of us!  

Read Marssel recap blog post here 

New York City Meetup 

Jumping to North America, SAP Champion Sebastiano Marchesini has recently announced the plans for a New York City community meetup. With warmer days arriving after the long winter, Sebastiano is inviting members to share their preferences on date, time, and format - whether weekday evenings, friday happy hour, or weekend afternoons. Suggested locations include outdoor options like Central Park or spots in New Jersey, as well as rooftops, pubs, or coffee places. If you are in the area around the next couple of months, please make sure to comment your suggestions on his blog post and help collaborating for this great opportunity to connect with other members.  

Read Sebastiano announcement blog post here 

SAP Inside Track North Rhine-Westphalia  

In Germany, preparations are underway for SAP Inside Track NRW (North Rhine-Westphalia), taking place on June 13 in Dortmund at adesso, with a full-day agenda starting at 9:00 AM. The event is part of a two-day experience, beginning with an SAP CodeJam on June 12 at UNIORG, focused on "Latest ABAP Cloud and RAP Features", followed by an evening SAP Stammtisch at Hövels Brewery. The SAP Inside Track agenda features a strong lineup of speakers, including Andre Fischer, Daniel Schlachter, Karin Meyer, Sebastian Werner, and Martin Pankraz, covering topics such as RAP, CAP, AI, Fiori, and Integration Suite. And the event concludes with a networking afterparty as well. All the details are already published on the Düsseldorf-Köln local group and you can already register for the SAP Stammtisch, the SAP Inside Track, and the dinner via  the link: registration.  

Read Björn announcement blog post here

entry.title: MRP Settings - MDBT creating Planned Orders then Purchase Requisitions
entry.content:
I have a scenario where our batched MRP settings for the weekend replenishments are not working as intended.  We have three plants with a daily and weekly MRP for each.

Settings for the weekly MRP:
NETPL
Create purchase req.  2
Schedule lines 1
Create MRP list  1
Planning mode  1
Scheduling  1

Settings for daily MRP:
NETCH
Create purchase req. 2
Schedule lines 3
Create MRP list 1
Planning mode 1
Scheduling 1

There are a few materials where the requirement is changing back and forth between planned order and purchase requisition every Saturday and Sunday. 
- I've confirmed the material has been entered into the planning file.
- Material has identical configuration to correctly MRP affected materials.
- Material is within the planning horizon.

Since this weekend MRP activity is flipping the material between planned order and purchase req, the purchase requisitions have no aging and appear newly released.  The material we're using to test the issue is planned with V1 replenishment as a spare in one plant, but resides as a production order component in another plant.  Despite the difference in planning, the purchase requisition is still flipping its creation status.

Can anyone suggest what I should be looking for that may cause this MRP issue?

entry.title: SAP Green Ledger: Q1-26 Release Updates and Highlights
entry.content:
With this blog post, I would like to introduce a regular quarterly update series highlighting the latest innovations and enhancements in SAP Green Ledger. These updates provide an overview of new features and improvements delivered during the last quarter, helping you stay up to date with ongoing developments.

Short introduction: What is SAP Green Ledger? SAP Green Ledger supports organizations in integrating carbon into financial processes. It runs as an application on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and integrates with SAP S/4HANA Cloud–based finance systems. It enables organizations to monitor and document carbon emission impacts at transaction level and link emissions to financial objects. By using established financial structures such as accounts, company codes, and controlling objects, carbon emissions can be consistently assigned to business activities and financial categories. This supports transparent and auditable reporting, as well as data-driven operational and strategic decision-making.

With Q1 2026, SAP Green Ledger continues to evolve, supporting organizations in moving beyond emissions tracking toward managing carbon as a financial dimension. The features released in the first quarter of this year bring a range of enhancements in carbon liability management, regulatory readiness, and financial integration, along with improvements that make everyday processes more transparent and flexible. This blog post covers the following new features:

  • CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities
  • Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates
  • Linking Carbon Data to Projects
  • Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems
  • Posting on Blocked Accounts
  • Reversing Manual Journal Entries
  • Further Updates and Improvements

 

CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities

One of the key highlights this quarter is the support for carbon certificate data and carbon liabilities. Carbon certificates and liabilities play a central role in regulatory frameworks such as the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), where companies must cover embedded emissions with certificates that carry a financial value. Since the EU CBAM came into effect in January 2026, companies that import specific goods into the EU are now required to report embedded emissions in these imported goods and build up corresponding carbon liabilities. While the actual purchase of EU CBAM certificates will only begin in 2027, organizations already need to track and value these obligations today.

To support these new requirements, the new Configure Carbon Certificate Data app provides a centralized entry point for maintaining the data required for carbon certificate handling and valuation. You can create and maintain Emissions Trading System (ETS) prices, which represent the cost of emitting one metric ton of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e). Since these prices fluctuate over time and directly impact liability valuation, maintaining them each calendar week helps ensure accuracy and alignment with market developments.

Maintaining Weekly EU ETS Prices (Illustrative Values Only)

In addition, you can display available company codes and access yearly CBAM factors. These factors determine the portion of embedded emissions that must be covered by CBAM certificates. By bringing ETS pricing, company context, and CBAM factors together, the app creates a consistent foundation that connects configuration with carbon data collection, liability calculation, and journal entry processing.

The flow is further supported by enhancements in the Manage Carbon Collections app. When creating a carbon collection, you can now indicate whether carbon certificates are required and maintain relevant information such as the number of certificates to surrender. This ensures that certificate requirements are captured consistently at the point of data entry. The information can then be used in later processing steps.

Carbon Certificate -relevant Carbon Collection

Building on this, the new Manage Carbon Liabilities app provides a dedicated space to view and evaluate carbon liabilities resulting from carbon collections that require certificates. You can analyze both the number of certificates to surrender as well as their corresponding monetary value based on ETS prices, reflecting the financial obligations associated with embedded emissions.

A key part of this process is fair value calculation. The system now calculates the fair value of carbon liabilities automatically for the current calendar year. This calculation is triggered whenever ETS prices are created, updated, or deleted, as well as when carbon collections requiring certificates are posted to the journal. Fair values are calculated by multiplying the number of certificates to surrender by the latest quarterly average ETS price. The calculated fair value covers the period from January 1 to the selected posting date within the year. At the same time, you can still trigger fair value calculations manually. This allows you to recalculate values when needed while keeping posting logic consistent.

Transparency is further improved through integration with journal entries as in the Manage Journal Entries app, you can review data originating from carbon collections. This includes, next to the line items for the collected GHG emissions, CBAM embedded emissions and certificate quantities now.

Journal Entries for CBAM-relevant Carbon Collection

Together, these capabilities provide greater transparency and control over how carbon liabilities are valued and reflected in financial processes. For more information, check SAP Help: Carbon Certificate Management. Looking ahead, we will continue to further develop support in this area. While the current focus is on supporting the calculation and management of carbon liabilities, future developments will address the next phase of EU CBAM. Starting from 2027, companies will be required to purchase CBAM certificates to cover their reported emissions. To support this, SAP Green Ledger will be extended with dedicated certificate management capabilities.

 

Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates

To support CBAM-relevant journal entry postings, account determination for carbon certificates has been enhanced with the Determine Mandatory Accounts app (formerly Manage Carbon-Only Accounts). It enables you to assign specific CBAM accounts to charts of accounts and company codes, supporting transactions such as liability recognition and fair value adjustments. This setup is mandatory for organizations subject to EU CBAM requirements. The app provides predefined CBAM account types for assets, liabilities, and operating expenses, which are typically maintained in a connected finance system and used in SAP Green Ledger. Postings to these accounts are generated automatically, ensuring alignment with underlying carbon processes.

Accounts Assignment for EU CBAM Accounts

Check out SAP Help for more information: Assign Carbon Certificate Accounts.

 

Linking Carbon Data to Projects

Carbon data can now be linked to projects and WBS (work breakdown structure) elements. A new WBS element field is available across the apps Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, Display Account Balances, and the Carbon Data Dashboard. This allows you to link carbon emissions directly to specific WBS elements. For that, project and WBS master data can now be imported from a connected finance system using our master data import capabilities and viewed in the new Display Projects app. This enhancement enables you to gain a more structured and detailed view of carbon data and makes it possible to analyze emissions at the level of individual projects and WBS elements.

Example Project in the Display Projects App

 

Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems

Another improvement is the increased flexibility in system integration with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management systems. Before, you could only connect one business system with the role SAP Sustainability Footprint Management to a SAP Green Ledger system. Now it is possible to create multiple business systems with that role. This allows you to work with more than one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system, for example when importing carbon data from different sources. At the same time, each finance business system can be associated with only one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system connected to a SAP Green Ledger system. This clear assignment ensures consistent and well-structured data integration. The setup is handled through business system configuration, where you define the system, assign its role, and maintain integration details such as the connected finance system.

 

Posting on Blocked Accounts

Posting behaviour has been made more flexible in SAP Green Ledger. Carbon collections can now be posted on accounts even if they are currently marked as blocked in the system. This is particularly relevant in scenarios where the account status may differ between SAP Green Ledger and the connected finance system. This can occur because account data in SAP Green Ledger is not updated in real time but is instead based on data imports from the finance system. As a result, the status in SAP Green Ledger may not always reflect the most current state.

Warning Message when Posting on a Blocked Account

Against this background, postings can still be carried out even if an account is indicated as blocked in SAP Green Ledger at a given point in time. Before posting, a warning message informs you if the account is marked as blocked. To ensure accuracy, you can verify the actual status of the account directly in the connected finance business system. Overall, this adjustment allows you to continue processes without interruption while still maintaining transparency and control over account usage.

 

Reversing Manual Journal Entries

You can now reverse manual journal entries in our Manage Journal Entries app, which makes it easier to correct postings and maintain accurate carbon data records. Reversing journal entries is possible if certain conditions are met, such as having the appropriate source type and not having been reversed previously. Once reversed, the system automatically creates corresponding journal entries in which debits and credits are swapped, ensuring that the financial impact is fully offset while preserving traceability.

The reversal information, including status, who initiated the reversal and when as well as the reversed journal entries, is available in the journal entry document, allowing you to track changes and maintain compliance with accounting standards. This enhancement helps ensure data accuracy while supporting controlled correction processes within SAP Green Ledger. For more details, have a look at SAP Help: Reverse Journal Entries.

 

Further Updates and Improvements

In addition to the key capabilities described above, several additional enhancements have been introduced to improve usability and transparency:

  • A new Journal Entry Source Type field is now available in key apps such as Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, and Display Account Balances and provides visibility into how journal entries were created (e.g., through allocation processes, liability valuation, carbon collection postings, imports, or manual entries and reversals).
  • Updates to the allocation functionality include the renaming of the allocation type Manual Allocation to Periodic Allocation and related UI adjustments.
  • Certain allocation options have been streamlined, such as the removal of the allocation type Account Balance Allocation from the Display Account Balances app.
  • External commenting is now available in the User Guide, enabling direct feedback and collaboration, see also Blog Post.

 

Additional Information

If you want to dive deeper into SAP Green Ledger, the SAP Help Portal is a good place to start. It provides detailed information on concepts, configuration, and application usage. You can also check the What’s New section, which is updated with every release and gives a complete overview of the latest additions. To see what's coming next, the SAP Road Map Explorer offers insight into planned features and upcoming innovations. It is updated regularly and outlines how the solution will continue to evolve.

entry.title: Re: How to dynamically determine Warehouse Source Storage Type by Customer for the same material cod
entry.content:
Hello @DahanChoi ! How are you sir?

I totally get the dilemma here, but I’ll be straight with you: this idea of creating a Storage Type per customer is a technical and logistical trap. As you might have noticed the Stock Removal Control Indicator in the Material Master is unique. Trying to force SAP to choose different paths based on the customer of the Sales Order would require lots of customization and I'm not even sure if it's possible. Furthermore, maintenance would eventually become a nightmare: even if you have 5 customers today, let's suppose it grows to 50 in the future. Imagine the amount of storage types to configure, monitor and inventory!?

Your customer might like you today for solving the problem, but certainly tomorrow they will cast stones at you! 😞

Storage types are meant to represent physical / logical subdivision of a warehouse defined by specific storage techniques, spatial characteristics, or organizational functions. If you take a look at some of them that are pre-delivered by SAP you're going to see "high-level rack", "staging area" and "fixed bin storage". These are good examples or best practices because they organize goods by physical handling and process flow, not by business attributes.

Taking the "high-rack" as a practical example, you can't put a 2-ton pallet in a small pick bin, and you can't put a small screw in a high-reach rack without losing efficiency in your warehouse. This storage type enforces the system to respect the physical geometry of the building so if you create a "Customer A" storage type, it doesn't tell the system anything about whether the product fits on a shelf or the floor. Examples go on and on and on...

I'd like to help you more about the problem you're trying to solve, however I'd like you to explain a little bit better about the process. What is the real reason for this separation? Is it a legal/tax requirement for physical segregation, is it consigned stock, or is it just to make things easier during inbound delivery and shipping?

Thanks a lot

entry.title: Access Control in ABAP CDS Using User Defined Aspects
entry.content:
In high-security environments, standard PFCG roles often aren't granular enough. Today, we’ll look at a sophisticated way to implement Row-Level Security using User-Defined Aspects. We'll walk through a real-world scenario: restricting Purchase Requisition items so users only see data for their authorized cost centers.

Now there's no such authorization object exists in SAP.

Step 1: Create the Authorization Source

First, we need a "Truth Table" that maps users to cost centers. We'll wrap our custom table (zp2p_cc_user) in a CDS view.
Note: To use a view in an Aspect, you must declare it as audited using the @AccessControl.auditing annotations.
@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Cost Center and Authorized Users'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

@AccessControl.auditing.type: #CUSTOM
@AccessControl.auditing.specification: 'Custom cost center authorization table audited via logging'

define view entity ZI_CostCenterUser
  as select from zp2p_cc_user
{
  key kostl as CostCenter,
  key uname as UserName
}

Step 2: Extend the Target View

Standard views like C_PurReqnItemMntr might not expose the CostCenter field by default. We use a CDS Extension to inject this field without modifying the standard SAP object.
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewAppendName: 'ZIEXTPRMNTR'
@EndUserText.label: 'Extension for Cost Center'
extend view C_PurReqnItemMntr with zi_ext_c_purreqnitemmntr

  association [0..1] to ZI_PRCostCenter as _PRCostCenter on _PRCostCenter.PurchaseRequisition = $projection.purchaserequisition

{
  _PRCostCenter.CostCenter as CostCenterAcc
}

Step 3: Define the Access Policy and Aspect

This is where the magic happens. An Access Policy is a specialized DCL object that defines a "Generic Aspect." It effectively tells the system: "Whenever I need to know a user's cost centers, run this specific query."
To create ACCESSPOLICY,  you need to create a ACCESS CONTROL in ADT and remove the generated code.
Syntax Tip: Use WITH USER ELEMENT to bind the current system user to your table's username field.
Note: Aspect name and the Access Control name should be same.
If you encounter a "Name must be identical" error, remember that in some versions of ABAP 7.5x, the Access Policy name  and the Aspect name are required to be identical if they are in the same file.
 
@EndUserText.label: 'Aspect for User Cost Center'

define accesspolicy ZV_UserCostCenter { 
    define aspect ZV_UserCostCenter as select from
        ZI_CostCenterUser with user element UserName
        { 
            CostCenter
        }
}

Step 4: The Final Access Control (DCL Role)

Finally, we apply this rule to our Purchase Requisition view. Instead of a static value, we use the ASPECT keyword to dynamically pull values from our Access Policy.
 
@MappingRole: true
define role ZI_PURCHASEREQUISITIONITEM {
    grant select on C_PurReqnItemMntr
    combination mode AND
    where ( CostCenterAcc ) = ASPECT ZV_UserCostCenter;
}

Why Use Aspects?

  • Centralized Logic: Change your authorization table or logic in one Aspect, and every role using that Aspect updates automatically.
  • Performance: The ABAP CDS engine converts this into a highly optimized database join at runtime.
  • Flexibility: It allows you to build complex, table-driven security that standard PFCG authorization objects simply can't handle.
This blog was written with the help of AI, there might be some syntax issues or grammatical errors.

entry.title: Re: How to Guide - Principal Propagation in an HTTPS Scenario
entry.content:
Hi @AntalP ,

 
After the SCC 18 → 19 and SAP JVM 107 → 109 upgrade, principal propagation fails on the landscape behind the 7.53 Web Dispatcher because the runtime backend TLS connection is not mutually authenticated. The Web Dispatcher trace shows NO client cert, and the SCC JSSE trace shows a successful TLS session without evidence of client-certificate presentation. This means the first failing point is the SCC-to-Web-Dispatcher mTLS step, not ABAP ICM.
 
[Thr 139974848579328]   New session (TLSv1.2, TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256{c0,2f})
[Thr 139974848579328]   (failed resume)  new TLS session_id="4AB01BCB D7CB78B6 3618A601 3DCF28B1 7ACF7D01 4CE65F94 ED9EC6CA 379A3658"
[Thr 139974848579328]   SapSSLISessionStartFin(sssl_hdl=7f4e40074410)==SAP_O_K
[Thr 139974848579328]  in/out: status = "new SSL session,TLSv1.2,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, NO client cert"
 
SCC Trace
 +0200#DEBUG#com.sap.core.connectivity.spi.processing.OutboundConnectionErrorHandler#tunnel-client-50-1#0xf2fd81f6#Protocol processing error:
com.sap.core.connectivity.protocol.http.handlers.HttpProtocolException: Principal propagation forbidden for user xxxxxxx on system sapgw.xxxxxx.xxx443, because the backend connection is not mutually authenticated.
at com.sap.core.connectivity.protocol.http.handlers.HttpAuthenticationHandler.validateMutualAuthentication(HttpAuthenticationHandler.java:248)
at com.sap.core.connectivity.protocol.http.handlers.HttpAuthenticationHandler.lambda$doAssertMutualAuthentication$0(HttpAuthenticationHandler.java:237)
at io.netty.util.concurrent.DefaultPromise.notifyListener0(DefaultPromise.java:604)
 
https://help.sap.com/whats-new/cf0cb2cb149647329b5d02aa96303f56?Component=Connectivity
says that "If mTLS is not used at runtime when using Principal Propagation or Technical User Propagation, the Cloud Connector now fails with an exception early to avoid late failures and insecure setups for those scenarios."
However , I am not sure what is actually we are missing? We have icm/HTTPS/verify_client = 1 in the web Dispatcher already.
 
Any pointer please. Raise a case with SAP already but no response yet.
 
Regards,
Mofizur

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite API Management Meets Agentic AI
entry.content:
Learn how our MCP Server turns SAP Integration Suite API Management into an autonomous, self-operating platform - from API creation to developer portal publication.

For over a decade, enterprise API management including in SAP Integration Suite has followed a familiar pattern. Requirements arrive, architects design the API, and developers create API proxies, configure endpoints, and apply security and traffic policies. The API is then deployed, published to the developer portal, and documented for consumption. While accelerators such as templates, reusable policies, and CI/CD pipelines streamline parts of this workflow, the lifecycle still depends heavily on human intervention at each stage.

We asked a different question: what if the platform could execute itself?

The answer is our MCP Server for SAP Integration Suite. Connect any MCP-compatible AI client -  Claude Desktop, Cursor, VS Code agents, or a custom-built agent, and SAP Integration Suite API Management becomes an autonomous, self-operating system. It interprets your intent, plans a sequence of actions, executes them, and corrects itself when something goes wrong while deploying. No scripts. No manual clicks. No back-and-forth between a developer and a UI.

What Is MCP - And Why Does It Matter Here?

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI models connect to external tools through a standardised interface. An MCP Server exposes callable tools. Any compatible client can discover and invoke them through natural language.

Our MCP Server exposes the operational depth of SAP Integration Suite API Management as AI-callable tools. The user describes an outcome. The agent does the work.

Autonomous API Lifecycle - End to End

SAP Integration Suite already covers the complete API lifecycle. Our MCP Server handsover that lifecycle to an AI agent. Here is what a single natural language instruction triggers:

API Creation

The agent selects between API as URL (lightweight passthrough), API as Proxy (fully governed intermediary), or API with API Provider (backend configuration with path mapping and version management). It reasons about what the user described and constructs the correct combination automatically.

API Policy Generation

Policies are not selected from a fixed menu. The agent reasons about your security and governance requirements based on the natural language input and constructs the right combination from the four policy groups – security policies, traffic management policies, mediation policies and extension policies.

Deployment to the API Gateway

The agent creates the API with desired API policies, sets deployment targets based on the provided input, creates API product and associates the product with the proxy, and activates the runtime, sequencing each operation correctly. If any step fails before deployment, it detects the failure, diagnoses the cause, and retries with a corrected configuration autonomously.

Developer Portal Publication

This is not a manual step left for later. The agent publishes to SAP API Business Hub as part of the same workflow. The API is discoverable by consumers the moment the agentic operation completes with no human handoff required.

Documentation Generation

Structured documentation covering endpoints, request and response schemas, authentication requirements, and usage policies is generated from the API specification and policy configuration, then published alongside the API. Documentation is not an afterthought, it is part of the agentic workflow.

The Self-Correcting Loop

The agent reviews its own work. It checks whether the API is active and reachable, policies are correctly enforced, the proxy returns expected responses, and the portal entry is live. Misconfigurations are diagnosed and corrected autonomously in the same operational cycle before deployment.

What This Means for SAP Customers

The practical implications are significant across four dimensions:

⚡  Speed

Hours of experienced consultant time - creation, policy, deployment, publishing to portal, documentation - compressed into minutes from a few natural language instructions.

■  Consistency

Policy application, naming conventions, documentation structure, portal publication, the agent handles all of it the same way on every single API.

↑  Scale

Teams bottlenecked on API management capacity now operate at a fundamentally different scale. Human experts focus on strategy and architecture decisions that genuinely require human judgement.

↺  Self-Healing

Deployment and publication failures resolved autonomously in the same cycle. The agent finds the problem, corrects it, and confirms resolution.

Connecting Your MCP Client

Any MCP-compatible client can connect. For Claude Desktop, add the server to your MCP settings with your SAP Integration Suite tenant credentials - that is it. The client discovers the full tool manifest automatically. Cursor and VS Code agents follow the same pattern. Custom agents built on any MCP-compatible framework can introspect the tool manifest and call tools directly.

Supported Clients

Claude Desktop  ·  Cursor  ·  VS Code Agent  ·  Custom MCP Agents  ·  Any MCP-compatible framework

Security and Governance — How the MCP Server Is Safeguarded

A natural question from any enterprise team evaluating agentic AI is: how is this secured? What flows through the MCP client, and how is the MCP Server itself protected? The answers are concrete and technically enforceable.

MCP Server built-in authentication

The MCP Server itself validates the connecting client using Basic Auth or Oauth 2.0. This is the first gate - an unauthenticated client cannot see what tool sever exposes.

What travels through the MCP client conversation

When a user issues a natural language instructions, the MCP client sends that instruction to the MCP Server after authentication. The MCP Server then issues instructions to SAP Integration Suite based on the user inputs. No actual business data flows through this channel. No order records, no customer data, no backend payload contents pass through the conversation. What the user describes is intent - configuration instructions, not sensitive data.

SAP Integration Suite credentials

The credentials the MCP Server uses to authenticate against SAP Integration Suite - tenant URL, client ID, and client secret are stored in the server configuration, not passed through the conversation. They never appear in the MCP client chat. Access to the MCP Server configuration should be governed with the same controls applied to SAP Integration Suite administrative access.

How the API proxies themselves are protected

Every API proxy the agent creates is secured by the policies the agent applies - OAuth 2.0, API key verification, JWT validation, IP allowlisting, quota enforcement, and threat protection. These are the same policy controls a senior integration developer would configure manually, applied consistently on every API, without exception.

Interested in understanding more. Reach out.

entry.title: RAP Analytical Tables
entry.content:
RAP Analytical Tables

Dear ABAP Community,

If you've ever built a Fiori app with reporting, you know how much setup the old analytics stack required. Cubes, dimensions, analytical queries, all wired together through the Analytical Engine. It worked, but you paid for it in modeling effort.

RAP Analytical Tables skip most of that ceremony. Introduced with SAP BTP ABAP Environment 2511, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2027, they run on OData V4, sit inside a regular RAP Fiori Elements app, and all it takes is a projection view with a few annotations. No cubes, no dimensions, no Analytical Engine.

This post walks through the setup, the CDS annotations that matter, and the limits you'll run into. Code included.

So What Exactly Is This?

A RAP Analytical Table is a Fiori list report that can do math. Sums, minimums, maximums, averages, and distinct counts. The numbers show up right in the table, no need to export to Excel or jump into a separate analytics tool.

Under the hood, it's a projection view annotated with @odata.applySupportedForAggregation: #FULL. OData V4 picks that up, enables the aggregation features, and Fiori Elements renders the right table control automatically.

There are catches, though. You can't hook into the processing with calculation, filter, or sort exits.

Five Aggregate Functions

You get five options:

 

Annotation
What it does
#SUMAdds up values. Pick a data type that won't overflow.
#MAXHighest value. Works for dates too.
#MINLowest value. Also works for dates.
#AVGAverage. Use a precise numeric type or you'll get rounding surprises.
#COUNT_DISTINCTCounts how many different values exist for a referenced element.

One thing to keep in mind: each element can only carry one aggregation. Need both sum and average for the same field? Create two elements, one for each.

COUNT_DISTINCT Is a Bit Different

This one needs extra attention. Unlike the other four, #COUNT_DISTINCT requires an @Aggregation.ReferenceElement annotation that points to the field you want to count.

Say you want to know how many different currencies appear in your data:

@Aggregation.default: #COUNT_DISTINCT
@EndUserText.label: 'Different Currencies (#COUNT_DISTINCT)'
@Aggregation.referenceElement: ['CurrencyCode']
differentCurrencies,

Here's the gotcha: on a single, ungrouped row, the count is always 1 regardless of the actual value stored in the field. The field's own content is completely ignored.

Note: Because of this, add a dedicated field for the count. If you reuse an existing field, the values on ungrouped rows will confuse everyone.

What It Looks Like

Once the annotations are in place, Fiori Elements does the rest. Totals, averages, counts show up directly in the list report, either as a summary row at the bottom or inline per column.

RAP analytical table with aggregated data: totals per currency and a distinct count of 4.

One thing that tripped me up at first: Fiori groups by whatever columns are currently visible. Behind the scenes, that's the $select in the OData request. So if you add a column, the grouping changes.

To lock the grouping to specific key fields instead, use:

@UI.presentationVariant: [{
  requestAtLeast: ['TravelUUID']
}]

That tells Fiori to always include TravelUUID in the grouping, regardless of which columns are on screen.

The same annotation also supports groupBy and sortOrder if you want to define a default grouping and sort order upfront.

Users can also group manually through the app settings or by right-clicking a column header.

Setting It Up

You need a read-only business object exposed through an OData V4 UI service. If you need an editable variant, that is currently in the works. Check the ABAP Platform Roadmap.

The example here is based on /DMO/A_TRVL_ANA from the ABAP Flight Reference Scenario.

Three steps.

1. Interface View

Your foundation. Fields you want to aggregate need to exist here. If you need MIN and MAX on the same source field, define it twice with different aliases. Currency fields need @Semantics.amount.currencyCode. For COUNT_DISTINCT, add a dedicated element using cast(1 as abap.int4).

2. Projection View

This is where it gets interesting. Add @odata.applySupportedForAggregation: #FULL at the view level and tag individual elements with @Aggregation.Default:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Travel Analytics Projection'
@OData.applySupportedForAggregation: #FULL
define root view entity C_TravelAnalytics
  provider contract transactional_query
  as projection on R_TravelAnalytics
{
  key TravelUUID,
      AgencyID,
      CustomerID,

      @Aggregation.default: #SUM
      TotalPrice,

      @Aggregation.default: #AVG
      TotalPrice as AveragePrice,

      @Aggregation.default: #MAX
      BeginDate,

      @Aggregation.default: #MIN
      EndDate,

      CurrencyCode,

      @Aggregation.default: #COUNT_DISTINCT
      @Aggregation.referenceElement: ['CurrencyCode']
      differentCurrencies
}

3. Metadata Extension and Service

Define the UI layout (line items, selection fields, presentation variant), then expose it: service definition, service binding, publish.

That's it. Seriously. Most of the work is in the CDS annotations, and once those are right, the framework takes over.

The Fine Print

Let's be upfront about the limits:

  • Read-only. An editable variant is in the works. ABAP Platform Roadmap.
  • No exits. Currently, calculation, filter, and sort exits cannot be annotated.
  • No extensibility. CDS entities annotated for RAP analytical tables consumption are not enabled for extensibility.
  • The classic @analytics.query stack could do more: virtual elements, calculated measures, complex hierarchies. RAP Analytical Tables can't touch any of that.

But that's kind of the point. You trade flexibility for simplicity. And if all you need is a reporting table with sums, averages, and grouping, you'll be done before lunch.

RAP Analytical Tables do not cover every analytics use case. For virtual elements, calculated measures, or deep hierarchies, the classic @analytics.query approach is still the way to go. But for straightforward reporting with sums, averages, and grouping, the setup is minimal and the result is immediately usable.

entry.title: Re: Universal Allocation in SAP S/4HANA 2021
entry.content:
Hi SAP Team,

Thanks for the insightful blog on Universal Allocation in SAP S/4HANA 2021.

I have a question regarding the use of Universal Allocation in a planning scenario involving SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC).

In our project, planning data is maintained in SAC, where planners adjust values at Business Unit level. Instead of implementing complex allocation logic directly in SAC using Data Actions, we are exploring whether the allocation processing can be handled in S/4HANA.

Our idea is:

  • Perform planning and adjustments in SAC
  • Export the plan data to S/4HANA (ACDOCP)
  • Execute allocation cycles using the Universal Allocation app in S/4HANA
  • Consume the allocated results back in SAC for reporting

From a functional and architectural perspective:

  • Is Universal Allocation designed to support such plan data scenarios (ACDOCP) coming from SAC?
  • Are there any limitations or best‑practice considerations when using Universal Allocation for planning allocations rather than actuals?
  • Would SAP still recommend SAC allocations for this use case, or is shifting allocation logic to S/4HANA considered a valid and scalable approach?

The motivation is to reduce SAC model and Data Action complexity and leverage S/4HANA’s central allocation capabilities.

Looking forward to your guidance and practical recommendations. @daigo_imai

entry.title: SAP User Feedback Days 2026: Two days of listening, learning, & building real connection in Walldorf
entry.content:
On April 15 and 16, 2026, we welcomed end users and experts from our customers to SAP Headquarters in Walldorf for a two-day event built around one simple idea: We Create the Future. We can say with absolute certainty that the event was a huge success—and what made it special wasn’t just the agenda, the sessions, or the logistics. It was the people. The honesty. The energy. The shared curiosity. And the feeling—again and again—that when we sit together and talk openly, we move faster and we build better. It is a source of joy and pride for us to host this event at SAP.

What SAP User Feedback Days are really about:

  • To create a space where customers feel truly heard
  • To bring product teams and user research together with end users’ face to face
  • To dig deeply into real use cases and the reality of daily work
  • To challenge our assumptions (and sometimes our favorite ideas) in a constructive way
  • To build relationships that last beyond the workshops

Over the two days, we heard a consistent theme from customers: they value not only the chance to influence the future of SAP software, but also the human closeness—the ability to meet the people behind the product, and to meet other experts from other companies who “get it”.

Day 1: Deep dives with real end users—where insights become tangible

The first day was dedicated to hands-on user research and direct exchange on specific AI use cases coming from our product teams. Our user research experts worked side-by-side with customers and our product teams to explore real workflows, pain points, decision moments, and what “good” looks like in practice.

One of the most meaningful things we heard was that some customers said they didn’t realize SAP was truly listening in this way. This is so motivating for us all and to see this in action together —it’s exactly why these days matter. Trust is built in moments like these.

   

Kicked off with a keynote on "We Create the Future".

 

Intense, but very fun, sessions with our customers on AI use cases!

Day 2: Looking forward—co-creating what comes next

If day one was about listening deeply to current reality, day two was about looking forward together. Through interactive workshops and collaborative discussions, customers and SAP colleagues explored what the future should feel like: where user experience must evolve, what users need from SAP to succeed, and how we can design for the next set of challenges coming in business and from AI technology —not in isolation, but together.

 

Our User Research Event lead, Christoph, driving a session with customers.

   

One of our expert sessions on Fast with Fiori.

 

Expert sessions and interactive exchanges: making it easy to connect beyond “the session”

In addition to the research activities, customers also had the chance to join expert sessions and explore interactive topics, designed to give space for informal questions, deeper product conversations, and spontaneous discussions.

Many customers told us they were impressed—not only by what SAP is working on, but by how approachable the experts were. These exchanges helped customers see the “why” behind decisions, and helped our teams see where clarity, guidance, and experience design need to be even stronger.

It was also a pleasure to experience our product teams learn more about what is so important about their products and use cases and also sometimes challenge their assumptions and see a different way forward. Plus, it was amazing to see them take time to show our customers our wonderful campus and explain why we like working so much for SAP.

Why this matters: better UX starts with closeness, not distance

We often talk about “customer centricity.” SAP User Feedback Days are what it looks like when we practice it.

Thank you—truly

To our customers and end users: thank you for making the trip, for trusting us with your honest feedback, and for being willing to engage so openly. The event would not be what it is without you—and it genuinely wouldn’t be the same without your presence and energy.

To the SAP colleagues across user research, product teams, and all the experts who supported sessions and exchanges: thank you for the preparation, professionalism, and care you brought into every customer interaction. The way you showed up made a real difference to how customers experienced SAP.

We’re already looking forward to what comes next—and to continuing the conversation, not only at the next event, but in the everyday work between events.

Customer Voices

Just a few comments that I can share from the day from our customers

  • “I brought my business colleague with me, to show him as well…..to see his excitement when.. he was working through the app, it was very delightful for me.”
  • “SAP is really going into the AI direction, that can help us automate our processes, which is great for us.” 
  • “Whoever, might be invited in future, I will just recommend to.. use this opportunity to.. come into this exchange. …. it's a great thing.” 
  • “Thanks to SAP for the invitation and the open exchange — this is exactly the kind of practical AI innovation our industry needs.”
  • Want to see some more proof? Check out the LinkedIn buzz here!!!

 

What’s next

We’re now consolidating insights and findings from the two days and working with the teams involved to ensure what we learned translates into tangible improvements—both in the near term and in future product direction.

If you joined us in Walldorf: thank you again. And please keep the conversation going—we truly value it!

From a very happy Alex & Walli

And not forgetting thank you from our project dream team!

entry.title: Career and Talent Development Q&A EMEA/ Americas Series
entry.content:
Career and Talent Development Q&A EMEA/ Americas Series

Guest Speakers: 

  • Beatrix Petro

entry.title: Re: App - Accumulation Detail At Document Level
entry.content:
Dear all,

 

Please note that this app is used to display withholding tax line items when withholding tax documents are posted for a vendor who has been granted a TAN-based exemption.

With this app, you can view all documents posted for a vendor within a fiscal year in India. In cases involving TAN-based exemptions, the withholding tax percentage may vary for each document posted for the vendor. The total withholding tax base amount for all such documents is reflected in the Accumulation Amount column of the FIWTIN_ACC_EXAM table. You can then use the Show Accumulation Details at Document Level app to review the relevant details.

 

Please refer the below help documents for more information,

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/634261119fec4d58970471f2c4a9a740/459e300474404de7ae60a4127edbcac5.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=LATEST

 

Best regards,

SAP Support - S/4HANA Cloud

entry.title: The quiet cost of change
entry.content:
The best HR technology in the world means nothing if people are not using it.

Even when a solution like SAP SuccessFactors HCM is designed to be intuitive, employees can still hesitate - not because it is difficult, but because it is new, different, or just one more thing to figure out on a busy day.

This is not a technology problem. It is a people and change challenge.

No one stands up in a meeting and says, “We have decided not to adopt this system.” Honestly, it would be refreshing if they did. At least then you would know exactly what resistance you are facing as you roll out new technology. Instead, frustration and friction show up quietly in the form of delays, workarounds, repeated questions, or that one colleague who somehow becomes the unofficial help desk for everyone else. You know the one.

Change does not show up in manageable amounts anymore. It is continuous. A new feature. An updated workflow.  A new AI capability that will save time, if people use it.

Individually, none of this feels overwhelming. Together, though, it means we are constantly adjusting. This is even more apparent with HR solutions, where many of the tasks are infrequent. This is why adoption starts to slip.

When organizations introduce solutions like SAP SuccessFactors HCM, they are not just adding new features. They are asking people to work in new ways. And behavioral change does not stick because of a single training session or a well-written email.

It sticks when people feel confident as they complete a task or workflow.

If change is constant, support needs to be as well. A digital adoption platform can help.

How to help employees fully realize the value of your HR technology

1. Focus on clarity, not just change

People do not resist change. They resist confusion.

If employees are not clear on why something is changing or how it helps them, even the best rollout can lose momentum quickly. HR has a real opportunity here to connect the dots and make the value tangible in everyday work.

Bringing guidance into the flow of work makes a big difference, especially when it helps employees also understand the business context behind their tasks. With WalkMe for SAP SuccessFactors HCM, employees get contextual guidance directly in their familiar interface, eliminating the need to switch between systems or remember training materials.

Clarity builds confidence. And confidence is what drives adoption.

2. Use data to continuously improve

Adoption is not a finish line. It evolves over time.

HR teams benefit from understanding how employees are interacting with the system, not just whether they logged in. Where do people get stuck? Which steps take longer than expected? Where do they drop off completely?

WalkMe for SAP SuccessFactors HCM surfaces these patterns, giving HR visibility into user behavior.

This insight makes it easier to fine-tune processes and communicate more effectively. Over time, each new change feels less like a disruption and more like a natural progression.

3. Build trust in AI and automation

AI is becoming part of everyday HR work, from drafting job descriptions to suggesting goals or learning paths. But adoption depends on trust. If employees do not understand how something works, they are less likely to rely on it.

Giving people visibility into why recommendations appear and the ability to adjust or refine them goes a long way in building confidence.

The same applies to automation. When repetitive steps are handled automatically, it gives people time back for work that requires their input. Framing this as support, not replacement, helps reinforce the value.

4. Help people learn in the moment

Traditional training has its place, but it is no longer enough on its own. People need support in the moment, not just in advance.

Embedding short, relevant guidance directly into workflows allows employees to keep moving while they learn. It removes the pressure of having to remember everything and replaces it with the ability to figure things out as they go.

When learning feels natural, adoption tends to follow.

5. Reinforce the right behaviors over time

Adoption is not a one-time event. It builds through repetition. Even when employees understand a new process, it does not automatically become second nature. Especially in HR, where many tasks are done infrequently, it is easy to forget steps.

This is where reinforcement matters. Timely reminders, in-the-moment guidance, and small nudges help employees stay on track without slowing them down. Over time, those moments of support turn new ways of working into habits.

With solutions like WalkMe for SAP SuccessFactors HCM, HR teams can reinforce key actions directly within the workflow, making it easier for employees to follow the right process every time.

And that consistency is what ultimately drives long-term adoption.

Adapting at the speed of change

Change is not slowing down. But how organizations support people through it is what sets them apart. Digital adoption platforms help ensure that investments in solutions like SAP SuccessFactors HCM translate into real outcomes.

When HR focuses on clarity, trust, and continuous improvement, employees do more than keep up. They become part of driving change forward.

Ready to see these principles in action? Watch the Success Connect session, “Everything You Need to Know About WalkMe for SAP SuccessFactors HCM,” for practical examples and real-world insights from customers like TUI, Nestle, and Arla Foods.

Because at the end of the day, digital adoption is not about the technology. It is about people and helping them move forward with confidence, no matter how quickly things change.

entry.title: Re: RBP permission - Access to Non-secured objects for no...
entry.content:
Access to non-secured objects is typically an admin-level permission, and it should not be granted to end users.

If an object is being used to support a business process, it should be properly secured. this way you can run audit reports on who has access to what in SuccessFactors.

You’ll likely need to review the objects used in your configuration and determine, on a case-by-case basis, whether they should be secured.

Please review this links:

https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-platform/implementing-metadata-framework-mdf/list-of-mdf-core-objects?locale=en-US
https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2767161
https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-platform/implementing-metadata-framework-mdf/configuring-object-definition?locale=en-US
https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2538520

entry.title: Re: How to dynamically calculate IT0014 wage type (20% of...
entry.content:
At what point in your Payroll Schema is the employee’s contributory salary (Salaire Cotisable / Basic Pay Base) finalized?  Does the IT0014 recurring wage type still exist in the Output Table at that point?

1 - Modify the appropriate processing class (in V_512W_D) to ensure that the IT0014 WT is not cumulated and transfered to RT until the employee's contributory salary is finalized;

2 - Create a PCR that will transfer a copy of the employee's contributory salary into a temporary variable using Operation ADDWT& xxxx (where xxxx represents the employee's contributory salary WT), followed by ADDWT * (because you want to keep the initial WT in the OT);

3 - Create a PCR that will process the IT0014 WT, replace it's Amount by the Amount of the temporary variable and multiply that Amount by 20% (idealy using a Constant in V_T511K so that the % could easily be changed at a later date) and transfer the result back into the Output Table. 

4 - Add your 2 new PCRs into your Payroll Schema immediately after the point where the employee's contributory salary is finalized, and make sure that both these WTs end up in the RT.  Note that you could also add Function PRINT with Par2 = VAR into your schema after the temporary variable is created so that you can easily find it's value when you examine the Payroll Log of an employee.

entry.title: Cannot create Custom Fields for Material Document Header in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

We are working on an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition environment under SAP GROW, and we have encountered a limitation regarding Custom Fields at the Material Document Header level.

Our scenario:

We have a custom Fiori freestyle application that posts Material Documents via the I_MaterialDocumentTP RAP BO. In this application, the user enters an "Ingress ID" which is stored in the Reference Document / Material Document Header Text field (XBLNR / MaterialDocumentHeaderText). This Ingress ID is a key to a custom Z table that holds carrier/transport information (driver name, vehicle plate, tare weight, and gross weight). We need to display these four fields on an Adobe Form associated with the Material Document printout.

The problem:

To achieve this, we need to create four Custom Fields at the Material Document Header level so they can be populated during posting and later consumed by the Adobe Form. However, when we go to the Custom Fields app, the Business Context "Inventory Management: Material Document" (header level) is not available. We can only find "Inventory Management: Material Document Item", which only allows creating custom fields at the item level.

We also explored the available BAdIs:

  • MMIM_ITEM_PROVIDE_DATA — This BAdI works at the item level. We can successfully enrich item-level custom fields, but it does not give us access to header fields like the Reference Document or Header Text.
  • MMIM_HEAD_PROVIDE_DATA — This BAdI works at the header level and provides access to header data. However, since we cannot create custom fields at the header level (due to the missing Business Context), there is no target field to write the transport information to.

What we need:

We need a way to create custom fields at the Material Document Header level, or an alternative approach that allows us to store four carrier-related fields (transport name, driver, plate number, tare weight) at the header level so they can be consumed by an Adobe Form during the Material Document printout.

Our questions:

  1. Is the Business Context "Inventory Management: Material Document" (header level) planned for a future release? If so, which release or timeline?
  2. Is there an alternative extensibility approach to add custom fields to the Material Document Header in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition while remaining Clean Core compliant?
  3. Has anyone faced a similar requirement and found a supported workaround?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated. Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Need to have a seperate flexible workflow for PO amen...
entry.content:
Dear @Sudharmv,

In Public Cloud this is not supported in standard configuration. For Purchase Orders SAP flexible workflow evaluates the active workflows in the defined order and ensures that only one matching workflow is started. In addition when an already approved PO is changed and ordered again SAP starts a new workflow instance based on the restart/start conditions. Because of this design you cannot create a separate PO amendment workflow with the same preconditions but a different approver chain purely through standard configuration

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/b5b82d3b98144ec789862fd63bc6112d.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3053155

If the business requirement is to route amended POs to different approvers the supported Public Cloud approach is to introduce a differentiator for the amendment case. SAP supports defining custom procurement preconditions in the Manage Workflow apps and it also supports custom workflow agent determination through Badi MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2. With that design, the amended PO can be identified separately and assigned to a different approval path

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002767845

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2646400

Recommended approach is keep the standard PO workflow with restart enabled for relevant PO changes and add either a custom precondition or custom agent determination so that the amendment case becomes distinguishable from the original PO. Without such a differentiator, standard configuration alone cannot deliver separate approvers for amendments when the preconditions remain the same

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Problem reinstalling SAP ASE
entry.content:
We really need to see the actual error message and any related stack traces.

During the dataserver creation an errorlog should be generated for the new dataserver ($SYBASE/$SYBASE_ASE/install/<dataserver_name>.log).

The file size should be relatively small so you should be able to upload it as an attachment (either edit your original question, or start a new 'Answer' to your question)

If the file has grown sufficiently large (eg, trying to build the dataserver several times) then consider renaming the file, running the install/build again, and then upload the new *.log file.

In the meantime, if you are running into the snap:validate stack trace issue (as alluded to by Bart), this has been addressed a couple times in this community (eg, this Q&A).  The workaround in that linked Q&A (assuming you're experiencing the same exact issue) does not require going to a newer version of the ASE product.  [As for why you were able to successfully install the product before ... *shrug* ... hard to say without knowing more about the old vs new environments; could be a difference in the OS/kernel, a different ASE PL## version, ????]

entry.title: Re: Problem reinstalling SAP ASE
entry.content:
@oneeyeman1 you have a couple options for uploading a file:

entry.title: Re: Sales Order A2X OData V4 — Does a deep insert POST ro...
entry.content:
Dear @alexa1996,

For Sales Order (A2X) OData V4 in SAP_COM_0109 the safe standard position is that a single deep insert POST should be treated as all-or-nothing. SAP Public Cloud API documentation states that for deep insert requests the entities are processed sequentially and SAP documents Continue on Error only for the separate Sales Order Simulate (A2X) API, not for the real create API. Based on that standard design, if one item fails validation in the actual create request the request should be treated as failing as a whole, not as header saved with only valid items

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/7c1c960180ac43378c042e1cd4fe2f28.html

There is no standard configuration documented for Public Cloud that changes this create behavior to partial persistence. The documented standard approach is to use Sales Order Simulate (A2X) first review the returned messages, and only then send the real create request

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/215bd15c8d354eea9bb47f18838ab43d.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/d4634b0c7f4f42698113ed362670c77f.html

Summary:

In standard Public Cloud deep insert create for Sales Order (A2X) OData V4 should be treated as transactional/all-or-nothing and there is no documented non-Badi option to save the header with only the valid items

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Access Control in ABAP CDS Using User Defined Aspects
entry.content:
This is really a valuable blog @NooruBohra   Just a add-on I feel will be good.

Developers often struggle when the data isn't filtering as expected. Adding a "How to Debug" section is extremely valuable:

  • SQL Trace (ST05): Show how to use ST05 to see the actual SQL statement generated. Developers can then see exactly how the  WHERE clause was expanded by the Aspect.

  • Authority Check Trace (STAUTHTRACE): Even though this is "User-Defined," the CDS access control still logs attempts here.

  • The NULL Pitfall: Mention that if the Aspect returns no values for a user, the user sees nothing by default. Providing a "fallback" or explaining how to handle "Super Users" who should see all data (using ASPECT User combined with PFCG) adds professional-grade complexity.

just adding it to be more informative.

entry.title: Re: Does PRIMARY KEY clustered check stored somewhere
entry.content:
@chris_keating, thank you.

 

I added the schema name and got the following query:

 

select
st.table_name as TabName
, st.clustered_index_id as ClusteredIdxID
, si.index_name as IdxName
, 'Primary Key' as IdxType
from
sys.systab st join sys.sysidx si join sys.sysuser u
where
si.index_category = 1 -- primary key
AND st.table_name = 'leagues'
AND st.creator = u.user_id
AND u.user_name = 'dba'

 

I will mark it as resolved.

Thank you.
